ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Kashmiri Rogan Josh; closest parent 'meat curry (food product)' used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for Kashmiri Rogan Josh as a composite dish; 'Lamb meat, cooked' (A0CKM) used as closest parent anchor for the primary protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Kashmiri Rogan Josh or a sufficiently close lamb curry concept.

main_ingredients: whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Kashmiri Rogan Josh is approximately 300 g, comprising bone-in lamb pieces in a rich spiced gravy, served alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Curry (Rogan Josh) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports values for mutton curry / rogan josh as a composite cooked dish. Values adapted to reflect the Kashmiri variant with higher fat content from ghee and mustard oil and the characteristic Kashmiri spice profile. Per-100g values are consistent with ICMR-NIN mutton curry data (energy ~150–165 kcal/100g range for bone-in lamb curry with gravy). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for lamb curry (FDC ID 174972) for secondary validation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous food: lamb curry / meat curry with spices
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kashmiri Rogan Josh. GI estimated at 25 based on analogous meat curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Meat-based curries with minimal starch and high protein and fat content consistently show low GI (range 20–35). Carbohydrate content per 100g is 3.2g; GL per 300g serving = (25 × 9.6g carb) / 100 = 2.4. gi_category is 'low' per threshold rule (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg/100g; ~1140 mg per 300g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200g and request reduced salt preparation, as a standard 300g restaurant serving provides approximately 1140 mg sodium, approaching 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~25) and low carbohydrate content (~3.2g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kashmiri Rogan Josh has a very low glycemic index of approximately 25 and a glycemic load of only 2.4 per 300g serving, making it a suitable protein-rich main dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided it is consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee and lamb (~4.2g/100g saturated fat; ~12.6g per 300g serving) and moderate cholesterol (~62mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption of Kashmiri Rogan Josh due to its saturated fat content from ghee and lamb fat, which contributes approximately 12.6g saturated fat per 300g serving; choosing leaner cuts and requesting reduced ghee preparation can lower cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kashmiri Rogan Josh is a celebrated slow-cooked lamb curry originating from the Kashmir Valley of northern India, and is one of the signature dishes of Wazwan, the traditional Kashmiri multi-course feast. The dish is characterised by its deep brick-red colour, derived primarily from Kashmiri red chillies (Deggi Mirch) and dried cockscomb flower (mawal), rather than from tomatoes, which distinguishes it from other Indian lamb curries. The lamb — traditionally bone-in — is braised in a fragrant base of ghee or mustard oil with whole spices including green cardamom, black cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaves, along with ground fennel (saunf), dry ginger powder (sonth), and asafoetida (hing). Yogurt is incorporated to create a rich, velvety gravy. The flavour profile is aromatic, mildly spicy, and deeply savoury, with a characteristic warmth from the spice blend rather than sharp heat. Texturally, the lamb becomes tender and falls off the bone after slow cooking. Nutritionally, Kashmiri Rogan Josh is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with approximately 12.5g protein and 10.8g fat per 100g, and a very low carbohydrate content of 3.2g/100g, yielding a glycemic index of approximately 25. It is rich in iron, zinc, vitamin B12, and niacin from the lamb. The sodium content is moderate to high (~380mg/100g) due to salt and spice blends used in restaurant preparation. Clinically, the dish is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycemic load, but should be consumed in moderation by those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and saturated fat content from ghee and lamb fat.

Is Rogan Josh good for diabetics?

Is Kashmiri Rogan Josh gluten free?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for Kashmiri Rogan Josh as a named dish exists; values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton curry data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central lamb curry entries. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides a direct per-100g nutrient profile for this specific dish under this exact name.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN mutton curry source data. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source data. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb curry data (FDC ID 174972) as ICMR-NIN does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions for this dish. Trans fat is reported as 0.1g/100g reflecting naturally occurring trans fats in ruminant meat (conjugated linoleic acid), not industrial trans fats. The input record used the spelling 'Roganjosh' and 'Kasshmiri'; the canonical English name is standardised to 'Kashmiri Rogan Josh'. The dish is served at Kinara - Boat Quay, Singapore at SGD 21.00. Multilingual names provided for Indian cuisine context following Singaporean multilingual naming rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). GI estimated at 25 (low) based on analogous meat curry entries; GL per 300g serving = (25 × 9.6) / 100 = 2.4.
